policy_statement: Hiroshima City sets KPIs for cross-sectional measures: dispersed power introductions in city facilities (1 by FY2020; 8 by FY2030) and smart communities introduced (3 by FY2020; 10 by FY2030).
mechanism_description: The city sets KPIs to introduce dispersed power in city facilities and to introduce smart communities, supporting distributed and smarter energy systems that displace fossil-based electricity generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Distributed energy + smart energy management → ↓ fossil electricity generation → ↓ CO₂/CH₄ emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city deploys distributed power resources (e.g., on-site renewables, CHP, storage) in facilities and implements “smart community” systems (monitoring, controls, demand response, microgrids) to manage local generation and load.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Increased local low/zero-carbon generation and optimized dispatch/load shifting reduce net electricity drawn from fossil-dominated generators and can reduce curtailment/inefficiencies.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower lifecycle GHG emissions from the power sector due to avoided fossil fuel combustion for electricity supply (and potentially reduced peak fossil generation).
dominant_pathway_test: Deploying distributed/smart community energy systems most strongly reduces GHG emissions by displacing fossil-fuel electricity generation with lower-carbon local generation and optimized operation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Distributed/smart energy systems → reduced fossil generation → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While smart communities can improve energy efficiency (Resource Efficiency) and enhance outage resilience (Adaptation), the defining, largest climate effect across policies in this mechanism is decarbonizing electricity supply via fossil displacement.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “smart community” program were purely demand-reduction without changing generation mix, it could lean Resource Efficiency; if focused on islanding for disasters, it could lean Adaptation. However, the canonical mechanism explicitly includes fossil generation displacement via distributed energy deployment, keeping Mitigation primary.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that fits this mechanism—deployment of distributed energy and smart-community energy systems intended to displace fossil electricity—shares the same primary mitigation pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype deferral: Whether a specific policy instance is primarily solar PV, CHP, storage-enabled peak shaving, demand response, or resilience microgrids (and the magnitude of co-benefits) must be assigned based on policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Smart-community initiatives can be implemented for resilience (islanding, critical loads) or efficiency (demand management) without clear fossil displacement; classification should remain Mitigation only when the program materially reduces fossil generation/emissions as part of the mechanism definition.
key_indicators: “Key Performance Indicator (KPI)”; “Introduction no. of dispersed power in the city facilities”; “Introduction no. of smart communities”; “(cumulative total)”; “FY2020”; “FY2030”
co_benefits: Energy resilience/reliability and potential cost savings from local generation and smarter demand management
instance_edge_case_notes: “Dispersed power” could include non-renewable distributed generation (e.g., gas CHP), but in the assigned mitigation pathway it is treated as lowering net fossil electricity and emissions; the text itself provides counts/targets only, not technology mix or implementation instrument.
